transcript.transcript: Hi, my name is Thad Webster. I'm a participant in the America 250 in Idaho Applied Oral History Project. Today is the 7th of April 2026, and I'm discussing over the internet with John Bertram some of his memories of working in Boise's River Street district for development. This oral history is conducted with the support of the Idaho Historical Society and their story, a software company, as a part of the America 250 in Idaho commemoration. The Applied Oral History Project supports the Commemoration Commission to advance the ideals of the American Revolution - Equality, Liberty and Justice - through meaningful community engagement and recognition, appreciation and commemoration of Idaho's journey in America's legacy. John, firstly, let me say thank you so much for your time today. This interview will allow you to reflect on and share your memories and experiences about working in the River Street District. Through these questions, I hope to gain insights into what the organization you were associated with went through, what they faced in trying to help River Street develop a better neighborhood and environment. I'll begin the interview with some a question and that'll start our conversation. During the interview, I may adjust the order of some questions to allow for a natural flow of topics. I might ask unscripted questions to clarify your answers or to follow up on points you've made. So before we jump into the discussion, do you give permission to record this interview? Yes I do. Good. And can I have you start by stating and spelling your first and last name. John Bertram, J o h n B e r t r a m. Great. You passed that test. Thanks. I'm glad I don't have to take it. John, we know from previous discussions, that the way you ended up getting to Boise was a bit of a circuitous route, but it basically was the result of you joining the Volunteers in Service to America Vista program and getting assigned to work for Vista in a a poverty program in Boise, Idaho. And that assignment put you physically in the in the River Street district. Can you start by describing a little bit about joining Vista and getting trained and assigned to Boise? Sure. Well, um, I joined Vista, um, 1969 and, uh, was trained in, uh, New York. We lived with a family for two weeks. They wanted to see if we were, um, serious about our job, and I guess I passed that. Yeah. And I said, well, can I be in the, in one in the biggest city? So that helped me get into Boise. Yeah. Uh, two went up to northern Idaho and, uh, two were in Boise and one was over in Elmore County. And while you were working here, did you have, uh, any oversight from the Vista program? Was there a, like a district supervisor or leader were. Assigned to the L a R, which stands for Elmer and Ada County Community Agency, which was a federally funded agency. And they had a they had a hierarchy of people working there. And so it was there when we were vistas, uh, and it would be a great place to go down there and get a pop or a candy bar or something like that. So that was on grand. Um. Were you. Were you aware of who owned the pearl? I don't I don't have it down on my notes here. No I'm not. Um, they weren't they probably weren't local in the neighborhood, I guess. Well, they might have been. It was there for quite a few years. Um, so it might have changed, you know, hands a couple times. Um, and then the, the days of the local little grocery store were coming to an end too. When you put, when Kmart built soon after that buttress was also the right there. So grocery store. Grocery store compared to the little one would have a hard time competing both in price and in quantity and choice, you know? Sure. Um, we did have an interesting thing. Um. Um. For a couple of years, Dorothy Buckner Webb is her last name now, but operate a community center at Grand and Lovers Lane. So that little house on the corner. So she tried to do her best to help a few people, and that only lasted a couple of years. But, uh, she was an energetic woman who who wanted to get things done. Uh, African American woman. Um, another interesting story is one of a woman named Earline Browning who wrote Martin Luther King as he was going doing his work in the South. Um, what did he what did she offer Martin Luther King? Um, she, she sent him a few pairs of shoes for the, for the for the march. Oh. Yeah. How appropriate. Yeah. So, you know, she tried to do what she could in a way. She had some sort of some of her, her best nice shoes We're probably new. Yeah. Newish. Um, another woman that we knew is a family from our family was a woman named Emma Nesbit, who lived on South 14th and often babysat my daughter, Sarah, when I lived on 14th. And this is my. Sarah would often complain that the house was too hot. I don't know if you've run across older people that have the place way too warm, but that was that was the case there. So, you know, and I, I still own the house on 14th. My daughter in law lives there. And, uh, um, I went through a divorce with my first wife. It's funny how my friends now again, because so many years have gone by. Yeah. Susan. Um, and I had a daughter and so she lives in Boise and, um, so, um, I've seen Susan, you forget about the bad times and it's like, oh, hi. Yeah, yeah. We don't do anything together, but, uh. Um. Well, mentioning some of those locations in your, uh, memories there, uh, brings up. You know, as an outsider to Boise, uh, didn't grow up here. I'm still learning a lot. I'm fascinated with Boise's development. You know, as a place out here in the middle of nowhere. Um, one of the things that has struck me as, uh. Quite intriguing is a place in Boise called Lover's Lane, which has very few references in the modern day. You don't see a Lover's Lane sign. I have found evidence that Lover's Lane additions a building. Addition that was put forth to the City Council and approved and platted and all that, uh, is still used as reference for some of that land in between the river and the river street. But, uh, but that, that, uh, street or path that originated as lovers lane is hard to find for someone like me from Kansas, out of town, out of state. So where is this lover's lane? And, and I found it through a series of, you know, investigations, uh, saw where it was, uh, cut off from downtown in a city council action, uh, because people wanted to use 11th Street to cross those railroad tracks Instead of Lover's Lane, which apparently had crossed some of the tracks in its way. Going towards Front Street, where a lot of the tracks were. I found where it was cut off. It still had a name, but then I found that 45 of the residents around there, like in the 1920s. I think it was 1924, went to city council and petitioned to have the name changed from Lover's Lane because they thought that was too unbecoming a name for a street in their neighborhood. This would be, you know, getting people excited about the wrong thing, right? You don't you don't want to come down here to make love or whatever. So it changed then to Pioneer Street and, and then still, you know, a newcomer like me goes around on maps and drives around this the city and looks for. So where is Pioneer Street? And that is so hard to find. But I actually put it together. When I saw the address for the Payette Brewing Company and Restaurant, and still, because that entrance from River Street into the parking lot is Pioneer Street, and the address for Payette Brewing is like six, four, four pioneer. Yeah. So then I turned around and I just cited it back. You know, it goes across the street there at River Street and it's a walkway now. You know, it's not a street. And, and then I got around on the other side with, with grand and I saw that's where I saw that playground right there where Lover's Lane used to go. And then it was Pioneer Street. And now it's, it's kind of getting, you know, it's like all the bushes growing up around something. It's all of it's just getting kind of changed and it's complexity. Um, but that's, those are, uh, great memories for people. Yeah. Well, it's an interesting thing because it's a diagonal street, you know? Yeah. And there were quite a few lover's lanes across the country. It was a. Oh, yeah. It was a big concern. People knew about, you know, so it, it was a, it was an opportunity. I think, again, we got money from model cities. I'm trying to remember how we got that. There was always issues of where property started and ended in River Street, you know. Yeah, yeah. There was a clear alignment going down angular that way. Yeah. And uh, I'm trying to remember how, I mean, we ended up getting that paved and a little bit of signing. And you, you might you might almost miss it because it's, it's not right on the on the corner by the by the connector there, you have to go over a little bit and then down. Um hum. Um hum. Um, and so, um, but it's a pretty popular bike pedestrian access. Yeah. Get, get to the, if you're going to go to the river, that's the best, one of the best ways. And the, the city just did a wonderful large improvement on 11th Street from north of downtown to the river, right, to make it more pedestrian friendly, bike friendly and all that kind of stuff, which, which builds up just a natural corridor. It's a, it's a nice, uh, part of our recent improvements of the downtown. Yes. Yeah, yeah. And it's a part of river, the river Street district, you know, and a part of it, it could be certainly presented as a part of River Street development, so to speak. Well, before we end, I just want to ask you if you have any other memories of, uh, some of the people that you met there in the area when you moved in, in 69, worked there 70 through 74 or whatever. Um, some, some of the folks, you know, homes, uh, that you worked on that you haven't mentioned yet or I remember, uh. In another interview you mentioned, uh, Virgil and Bessie Stewart on the corner of 14th and River. Right? And he was a janitor for the art museum, and she cooked up the best pies. Um, and that was a pretty nice house is a two story house. It was one of the first ones we painted. I don't know if we talked about that, but, uh, we were able to get great paint supplies, ladders, etc. and I remember getting up on the I wasn't afraid of heights because I had tree forts and things. Uh, getting up on the painting part way up. It was two story. Yeah. You know. I had to get up underneath or. Right. Yeah. And we didn't really have all the stuff you'd have today with, uh, building it all up with this, you know, with the equipment and steel and things like. Yeah, scaffolding. Um, but, uh, so he worked at, he was the maintenance man at the Boise Art Museum. She was a lovely person who was friendly where we would have, we didn't, we had a few meetings at her house. Um, we would try to move those meetings around wherever we could. But when I had the River Street Design Center, we had our own place to meet and it was more comfortable to meet there without having to go in somebody's house. And so and. So. Where was the new Kmart? In location. reference to her house. Oh, well, if you just if you just, uh, 14th, if you just went straight down. 14th. Kmart's. Right. Right. Just to the right of that. Oh, okay. Yeah. So post office over here. Yeah. And then and then then Kmart. Okay. And as I mentioned, Buttrey's was we probably might have still had a couple of little grocery stores at Buttrey's hadn't have opened up a market supermarket there, but you couldn't compete. Yeah. So, um. And do you recall when you were there when you first got there? River Street, do you recall River Street on the northwest end ever crossing the tracks, or did it end right at the railroad tracks? It ended there. I think it pretty much ended there. There was so much. They used to used to have this crossing. They had crossing. They even had little crossing guards. And I mentioned that little booth. Yeah. So they would actually come out with a flag. And after as time went on, they. There were less trained activity there. And so they dispensed with that and the crossing guards and all that. Yeah. Um and that's where we got that little, little house. Yeah, yeah. Got the shed. But river wasn't ever uh, one that had a cross in there, I guess because there was so much. It was a yard. I mean, that was a large train yard. Yeah. It took a while for River to even get connected, you know? Yeah. You know, once river, um, once river was built, then you're really kind of your kids were stuck in the middle. You couldn't just say, I'm going down to the river or whatever, right? Yeah. Well, and and river on the east side runs right into Julia Davis Park, right? Exactly. Yeah. And so it kind of gets absorbed there. It's. Yeah, it was just dead end. Yeah. Well, this has been very enjoyable for me to get a nice history lesson from you, John, on River Street and well, here are your memories of it. Pleased to be helpful. And, uh, yeah, I think that's important to know all this history and to get it written down and, uh, uh, help me kind of just to get my thoughts together by putting some of this together. Yeah. So at some point in time, if you want to borrow this map. Yeah, I want to get by there. Quite. Uh, yeah. Uh, a good thing to have in the archives. Yep. Okay, great. Well, then I'll bring this to an end, and, uh, we'll chat with you after I get back from my trip. Okay. Where are you headed? New Zealand. Oh, wow. You think you mentioned that? Yeah. Hey. Have fun. Be careful. Okay. Thanks a lot. Yeah. Okay. Bye. Bye. Bye.

lines.line: # Introduction and Interview Setup

> The interviewer, Thad Webster, introduces himself and the America 250 in Idaho Applied Oral History Project, outlining the purpose and context of the oral history interview. He explains the project's goals, the support from the Idaho Historical Society, and the ideals it aims to advance. The interview setup includes obtaining consent to record and confirming the spelling of the interviewee's name, John Bertram, establishing a formal and respectful tone for the conversation.

metadata.title: Introduction and Interview Setup
metadata.synopsis: The interviewer, Thad Webster, introduces himself and the America 250 in Idaho Applied Oral History Project, outlining the purpose and context of the oral history interview. He explains the project's goals, the support from the Idaho Historical Society, and the ideals it aims to advance. The interview setup includes obtaining consent to record and confirming the spelling of the interviewee's name, John Bertram, establishing a formal and respectful tone for the conversation.

lines.line: # Joining Vista and Arrival in Boise

> John Bertram recounts his decision to join the Volunteers in Service to America (Vista) program in 1969, his training experience in New York, and the process of being assigned to Boise, Idaho. He describes his initial expectations for urban assignments and the reality of being sent to Boise, along with other volunteers. The narrative includes details about the assignment distribution and the initial steps taken upon arrival in Idaho.

metadata.title: Joining Vista and Arrival in Boise
metadata.synopsis: John Bertram recounts his decision to join the Volunteers in Service to America (Vista) program in 1969, his training experience in New York, and the process of being assigned to Boise, Idaho. He describes his initial expectations for urban assignments and the reality of being sent to Boise, along with other volunteers. The narrative includes details about the assignment distribution and the initial steps taken upon arrival in Idaho.

lines.line: # Early Activities in River Street: Painting Houses and Community Engagement

> The speaker describes settling into the River Street neighborhood, finding housing, and interacting with local families. He shares anecdotes about neighbors, including the Browning family, and details the group's efforts to paint houses in the area. These activities helped build trust and relationships with residents, demonstrating the volunteers' commitment to improving the neighborhood and fostering community engagement.

metadata.title: Early Activities in River Street: Painting Houses and Community Engagement

He shares anecdotes about neighbors, including the Browning family, and details the group's efforts to paint houses in the area. These activities helped build trust and relationships with residents, demonstrating the volunteers' commitment to improving the neighborhood and fostering community engagement.

lines.line: # Oversight, Housing, and Community Action Agency Work

> The discussion covers the oversight provided by the Community Action Agency, the process of finding permanent housing, and the initial challenges faced by the Vista volunteers. The speaker explains the agency's structure, the support received from local families, and the importance of establishing a base in the River Street district. The narrative highlights the flexibility and autonomy given to the volunteers as they began their work.

metadata.title: Oversight, Housing, and Community Action Agency Work

The speaker explains the agency's structure, the support received from local families, and the importance of establishing a base in the River Street district. The narrative highlights the flexibility and autonomy given to the volunteers as they began their work.

lines.line: # Food Commodities Program and Advocacy

> The speaker details the challenges faced in securing federal food commodities for the River Street community, including resistance from Ada County commissioners. He describes the advocacy efforts, organizing residents to demonstrate the need for assistance, and the eventual approval of the program. The narrative emphasizes the importance of food distribution and the impact of these efforts on the neighborhood's well-being.

metadata.title: Food Commodities Program and Advocacy

He describes the advocacy efforts, organizing residents to demonstrate the need for assistance, and the eventual approval of the program. The narrative emphasizes the importance of food distribution and the impact of these efforts on the neighborhood's well-being.

lines.line: # River Street's Ethnic Diversity and Social Dynamics

> The speaker provides a historical overview of River Street's ethnic diversity, listing various immigrant groups and explaining how the area became home to Boise's black population due to discrimination. He discusses the unique blend of cultures, the sense of community fostered by shared experiences, and the physical boundaries that shaped the neighborhood. The narrative highlights the challenges and resilience of River Street's residents.

metadata.title: River Street's Ethnic Diversity and Social Dynamics

He discusses the unique blend of cultures, the sense of community fostered by shared experiences, and the physical boundaries that shaped the neighborhood. The narrative highlights the challenges and resilience of River Street's residents.

lines.line: # Job Corps, Neighbor Relations, and Personal Experiences

> The speaker describes the presence of the Job Corps program in the area, interactions with young men from the program, and personal experiences with neighbors. He shares stories about his own drive and skills, learned from his family, and the relationships formed with local residents. The narrative includes anecdotes about babysitting arrangements and the everyday life of families in River Street.

metadata.title: Job Corps, Neighbor Relations, and Personal Experiences

The narrative includes anecdotes about babysitting arrangements and the everyday life of families in River Street.

lines.line: # River Street's Physical Boundaries and Playground Development

> The conversation shifts to the physical constraints of River Street, surrounded by industrial uses and lacking play spaces for children. The speaker recounts efforts to develop a playground, including negotiations with property owners, sourcing materials, and engaging children in tree planting. He reflects on the importance of community involvement and the lasting impact of these improvements, noting the playground's continued existence as a daycare center.

metadata.title: River Street's Physical Boundaries and Playground Development

The speaker recounts efforts to develop a playground, including negotiations with property owners, sourcing materials, and engaging children in tree planting. He reflects on the importance of community involvement and the lasting impact of these improvements, noting the playground's continued existence as a daycare center.

lines.line: # Purchasing a House and River Street's Early Development

> The speaker discusses buying a house in 1971, its poor condition, and the remnants left by previous owners. He transitions to the history of River Street's development, referencing early landowners, real estate developers, and the impact of the railroad and warehouses. The narrative explores the evolution of the neighborhood's layout and the challenges posed by industrial expansion.

metadata.title: Purchasing a House and River Street's Early Development
metadata.synopsis: The speaker discusses buying a house in 1971, its poor condition, and the remnants left by previous owners. He transitions to the history of River Street's development, referencing early landowners, real estate developers, and the impact of the railroad and warehouses. The narrative explores the evolution of the neighborhood's layout and the challenges posed by industrial expansion.

lines.line: # Flooding, Greenbelt Development, and Urban Planning

> The speaker describes flooding issues in River Street, the need for flood control, and the development of the Boise River greenbelt. He explains how the city was inspired by similar projects in Eugene, Oregon, and the process of clearing riverbanks for public access. The narrative highlights the importance of urban planning decisions in shaping the neighborhood's environment and recreational opportunities.

metadata.title: Flooding, Greenbelt Development, and Urban Planning
metadata.synopsis: The speaker describes flooding issues in River Street, the need for flood control, and the development of the Boise River greenbelt. He explains how the city was inspired by similar projects in Eugene, Oregon, and the process of clearing riverbanks for public access. The narrative highlights the importance of urban planning decisions in shaping the neighborhood's environment and recreational opportunities.

lines.line: # River Street Neighborhood Plan and Land Use Changes

> The speaker discusses the River Street neighborhood plan, including proposals for residential development, the impact of the interstate connector, and the shift toward office and commercial uses. He reflects on the influence of developers like Winston Moore and the loss of opportunities for residential growth. The narrative examines the broader context of urban renewal and the preservation of historic areas in Boise.

metadata.title: River Street Neighborhood Plan and Land Use Changes

He reflects on the influence of developers like Winston Moore and the loss of opportunities for residential growth. The narrative examines the broader context of urban renewal and the preservation of historic areas in Boise.

lines.line: # River Street Design Center and Housing Initiatives

> The speaker describes establishing the River Street Design Center, organizing community meetings, and addressing housing needs in the neighborhood. He recounts a bus tour of different housing types, efforts to secure federal funding, and the challenges posed by changes in government policy. The narrative highlights the importance of home ownership and the limitations of available resources for neighborhood improvement.

metadata.title: River Street Design Center and Housing Initiatives

He recounts a bus tour of different housing types, efforts to secure federal funding, and the challenges posed by changes in government policy. The narrative highlights the importance of home ownership and the limitations of available resources for neighborhood improvement.

lines.line: # Local Businesses, Community Centers, and Notable Residents

> The speaker provides details about local businesses, including grocery stores and their changing roles, as well as community centers and notable residents. He shares stories about individuals like Dorothy Buckner Webb and Earline Browning, emphasizing their contributions to the community. The narrative includes personal anecdotes about neighbors and the speaker's ongoing connection to the area.

metadata.title: Local Businesses, Community Centers, and Notable Residents

lines.line: # Lover's Lane, Pioneer Street, and Urban Geography

> The conversation explores the history and transformation of Lover's Lane, its renaming to Pioneer Street, and its current status as a walkway and bike corridor. The speaker discusses the challenges of identifying these streets in modern Boise, their significance in urban geography, and the city's recent improvements to pedestrian and bike infrastructure. The narrative reflects on the changing landscape and the preservation of historical routes.

metadata.title: Lover's Lane, Pioneer Street, and Urban Geography

lines.line: # Additional Memories of Residents and River Street's Layout

> The speaker shares additional memories of residents, such as Virgil and Bessie Stewart, and their contributions to the community. He describes painting houses, the location of key landmarks like Kmart and the post office, and the layout of River Street, including its crossings and connections. The narrative provides insights into the neighborhood's structure and the impact of industrial development on residential life.

> The interview concludes with expressions of gratitude, reflections on the importance of documenting history, and the value of preserving memories for future generations. The speaker offers to share historical materials and discusses plans for future contact, ending the conversation on a positive and collaborative note.

story.title: River Street VISTA - John Bertram
story.description: John's memories of VISTA program (War on Poverty) activities in Boise's River Street neighborhood - 1969-1974
